findings.title: Crash in buildEmailHtml when bills is not an array or amounts/total are not numbers
findings.description: In emailTemplate.js, `bills.map(b => ...)` is called unconditionally, and `b.amount.toFixed(2)` is called on each entry and `total.toFixed(2)` is called in multiple places — all without any type guards. In server.js, the POST /api/email/send and /api/email/test handlers take `bills` and `total` directly from `req.body` with no validation before passing them to `buildEmailHtml`. If `bills` is null, undefined, or not an array, the `.map()` call throws a TypeError. If any `b.amount` or `total` is a string, undefined, or missing, `.toFixed()` throws. Both handlers call `buildEmailHtml` before the try/catch that wraps `sendEmail`, so the crash propagates as an unhandled 500 response.
findings.recommendation: Validate in the /api/email/send and /api/email/test handlers that `bills` is an Array, `total` is a finite number, and each `bill.amount` is a finite number before calling `buildEmailHtml`; return a 400 on failure. Also add defensive guards inside `buildEmailHtml` itself: `const safeBills = Array.isArray(bills) ? bills.map(b => ({ name: String(b.name ?? ''), amount: Number(b.amount) || 0 })) : [];` and `const safeTotal = typeof total === 'number' && isFinite(total) ? total : 0;`.

findings.title: No timeout on outbound HTTP calls to email provider APIs — server hangs indefinitely
findings.description: The `post()` and `postForm()` helpers in email.js issue `fetch()` calls with no `AbortController` signal. If Mailgun, SendGrid, or Resend hangs, the Express route handlers for /api/email/send and /api/email/test will hold their HTTP connections open indefinitely. Under load, a stalled email provider can exhaust all open connections and render the entire application unresponsive. The same applies to `sendViaSmtp` via nodemailer, which has no explicit `connectionTimeout` or `socketTimeout` configured in the transport options.
findings.recommendation: Pass an `AbortSignal` with a 15-second timeout to every `fetch()` call using `AbortController`. For `sendViaSmtp`, add `connectionTimeout: 10000` and `socketTimeout: 15000` to the nodemailer transport options.

findings.title: Proxy auth headers trivially spoofable — any direct client can impersonate any user
findings.description: The authentication middleware in server.js unconditionally trusts `remote-user`, `x-authentik-username`, `x-forwarded-user`, and `x-remote-user` HTTP headers with no validation that the request actually arrived through a trusted reverse proxy. If the application is reachable directly on port 8080 (e.g. Docker port-mapping, misconfigured firewall rule, or during local dev), any unauthenticated client can inject `remote-user: victim` and gain full read, write, and delete access to that user's bills, monthly records, and email config including encrypted provider secrets. The app has no IP allowlist, signed-token mechanism, or startup warning to distinguish proxy-forwarded requests from direct ones. The fallback to 'local' only activates when none of the four headers are present — sending even one is trusted unconditionally.
findings.recommendation: Add a mandatory shared secret between the reverse proxy and the application — require an `X-Internal-Token` header whose value matches a secret set via environment variable, and reject any request where it is absent or wrong. Alternatively, bind the server to 127.0.0.1 instead of 0.0.0.0 so it is unreachable without going through the proxy. Both mitigations together are ideal. At minimum, emit a prominent startup warning when the secret environment variable is unset, and document the requirement explicitly.

findings.title: sanitize-html configured with allowedAttributes: false — event-handler attributes pass through unchanged
findings.description: The `EMAIL_SANITIZE_OPTS` in email.js sets `allowedAttributes: false`, which per the sanitize-html documentation allows ALL attributes on all permitted tags — including `onclick`, `onerror`, `onload`, `onmouseover`, and every other event-handler attribute. Combined with `allowVulnerableTags: true` (which permits `<style>` tags), the sanitizer provides substantially weaker protection than its presence implies. The comment `// Sanitize HTML to prevent injection — breaks CodeQL taint chain` is a warning sign that the sanitizer's primary function is to satisfy static analysis rather than to genuinely neutralise malicious payloads. The intent is to preserve inline styles for email clients, but the broad setting allows JavaScript event handlers through entirely. If any bill name, greeting, or other user-controlled field reaches `buildEmailHtml` with injected markup (e.g. via a compromised DB entry or a future code path), the sanitizer will not strip it and the payload will be delivered to recipients' email clients.
findings.recommendation: Replace `allowedAttributes: false` with an explicit allowlist covering every attribute needed for email rendering. For example: `{ '*': ['style', 'class', 'id', 'align', 'width', 'height', 'bgcolor', 'cellpadding', 'cellspacing', 'border', 'valign', 'colspan', 'rowspan', 'src', 'href', 'alt', 'title', 'target', 'rel', 'lang', 'charset', 'content', 'name', 'xmlns', 'viewBox', 'fill'] }`. This preserves inline styling and layout attributes while stripping all event handlers. Do not include `on*` attributes under any circumstances.

findings.title: SMTP TLS certificate verification unconditionally disabled — credentials exposed to MitM
findings.description: In `sendViaSmtp`, the nodemailer transport is created with `tls: { rejectUnauthorized: false }`. This disables certificate validation for every SMTP connection, regardless of whether the user has configured a public mail relay (Gmail, Fastmail, AWS SES) or a self-hosted one. An attacker who can intercept the network path between BillHive and the SMTP server — trivially possible on the same Docker network, LAN segment, or a compromised relay — can present a self-signed certificate, complete a successful TLS handshake, and read the SMTP credentials (`smtpUser`/`smtpPass`) and all email content in cleartext. The comment 'allow self-signed for self-hosted' does not justify disabling validation universally. This affects every deployment that uses SMTP regardless of the user's intent.
findings.recommendation: Remove `rejectUnauthorized: false` from the default transport config. Add an optional boolean field (e.g. `smtpAllowSelfSigned`) to the SMTP config schema and only set `rejectUnauthorized: false` when the user explicitly enables it: `tls: { rejectUnauthorized: !(cfg.smtpAllowSelfSigned === true) }`. Validate this field in the PUT /api/email/config handler so it can only be set intentionally, and display a visible warning in the settings UI when enabled.

findings.title: SSE client Sets never pruned from Map after disconnect — persistent memory growth and stale connections
findings.description: In server.js, `sseClients` is a `Map<userId, Set<res>>`. The `req.on('close')` handler removes the `res` from the Set but never removes the now-empty Set from the Map, so the Map grows indefinitely with a dead entry for every user who has ever opened an SSE connection. Additionally, there is no periodic heartbeat comment written to the stream. Reverse proxies commonly have idle-connection timeouts of 60–300 seconds; when a proxy silently drops a stale connection the close event may not fire, allowing dead `res` objects to accumulate in the Set and causing silent write errors in `broadcastChange`.
findings.recommendation: After deleting `res` from its Set in the close handler, check if the Set is empty and delete it from the Map: `const s = sseClients.get(req.userId); s?.delete(res); if (s?.size === 0) sseClients.delete(req.userId);`. Add a periodic `setInterval` heartbeat (e.g. every 25 seconds) that writes a comment ping to each `res` and removes any that throw, so stale proxy-dropped connections are detected and pruned promptly.

findings.title: Import endpoint accepts unvalidated month keys and silently overwrites all live user data
findings.description: The `PUT /api/months/:key` endpoint enforces `if (!/^\d{4}-\d{2}$/.test(key))` before writing to the database. However, `POST /api/import` iterates `Object.entries(monthly)` and calls `stmts.setMonth.run(userId, k, ...)` for every key with no format validation. Malformed or arbitrarily long month keys can be persisted to `monthly_data`, creating schema inconsistency that causes subtle downstream bugs when those keys are read back. Additionally, the import runs `INSERT OR REPLACE` unconditionally on all state and monthly keys inside a transaction, silently overwriting all live user data with no confirmation step or dry-run option. The 2 MB body limit provides only a coarse guard against large payloads.
findings.recommendation: Apply the same `/^\d{4}-\d{2}$/` regex to each month key inside the import transaction and skip (or return a 400 for) any key that does not match. Validate that `state` and `monthly` are plain objects if present. Add a key-count cap (e.g. 500 state keys, 120 month entries). Consider a `?dryRun=true` query parameter that validates the payload and reports what would change without committing.

findings.title: data: URI scheme globally allowed in HTML sanitizer — enables phishing and tracking pixels
findings.description: The `allowedSchemes` array in `EMAIL_SANITIZE_OPTS` includes `'data'`. Because `allowedAttributes: false` (see F-004) allows the `src` attribute on all tags, any `data:` URI can appear on any element. This enables: (a) inline JavaScript execution in email clients that support `data:text/html` or `data:text/javascript` URIs on anchors or iframes; (b) insertion of tracking pixels via `data:image/...` that bypass some privacy guards; (c) embedding of arbitrary binary content. Email clients vary widely in how they handle `data:` URIs and some older clients execute them.
findings.recommendation: Remove `'data'` from `allowedSchemes`. If inline images are genuinely needed for the logo or other assets, host them at a stable HTTPS URL or use Content-ID (CID) attachments via nodemailer instead of base64 data URIs. If `data:` URIs for images are strictly necessary, restrict them using `allowedSchemesAppliedToAttributes: { src: ['data', 'https'] }` rather than allowing `data:` scheme globally.

findings.title: Raw exception messages from email provider calls exposed to API clients
findings.description: Both `/api/email/send` and `/api/email/test` catch errors from `sendEmail()` and return `e.message` directly in the JSON response body. Errors from provider APIs and from nodemailer frequently contain sensitive details: SMTP server hostnames, partial credentials in authentication failure messages, internal network addresses, and provider-specific debugging information (e.g. a nodemailer SMTP authentication failure typically includes the server greeting, the attempted username, and the SASL response code). This information is not appropriate to surface to the browser.
findings.recommendation: Log the full error server-side (`console.error('Email send failed:', e)`) and return a sanitised, generic message to the client such as `'Email delivery failed. Check your provider configuration.'`. Only include a mapped human-readable category (auth failure, network timeout, etc.) in the response — never the raw `e.message` string.

findings.title: Unlimited concurrent SSE connections per user — denial of service via connection exhaustion
findings.description: The `/api/events` SSE endpoint adds every new response object to a per-user Set with no cap on the number of concurrent connections. Each SSE connection holds an open HTTP socket tracked in `sseClients` for the lifetime of the connection. A malicious or malfunctioning client can open thousands of simultaneous connections for the same `userId`, exhausting file descriptors and memory on the Node.js process. This risk is compounded by the auth spoofing issue (F-003), where an attacker can target any userId.
findings.recommendation: Add a per-user connection limit (e.g. 10) checked before adding the new `res` to the Set. If the limit is reached, send an error event and call `res.end()` on the oldest connection (or the new one) before returning. Apply a separate, stricter rate limiter specifically to the `/api/events` endpoint to prevent rapid reconnection.

findings.title: Rate limiter applied twice to SPA fallback route — effective limit halved for all frontend users
findings.description: In server.js, `app.use(limiter)` is registered globally so every incoming request already passes through it. The SPA catch-all route then applies it a second time: `app.get('*', limiter, (req, res) => {...})`. Every page load and navigation therefore consumes two tokens from the rate-limit bucket, effectively halving the configured threshold of 300 req/15 min to approximately 150 page navigations for regular users browsing the frontend.
findings.recommendation: Remove the inline `limiter` argument from the catch-all route: change `app.get('*', limiter, (req, res) => {` to `app.get('*', (req, res) => {`. The global middleware already covers all routes including the SPA fallback.

findings.title: Month key format not validated on DELETE, only on PUT — inconsistent API contract
findings.description: In server.js, `PUT /api/months/:key` validates the key against `/^\d{4}-\d{2}$/` and returns 400 on mismatch. `DELETE /api/months/:key` calls `stmts.deleteMonth.run` unconditionally with no format check. While this is a no-op in SQLite for non-existent rows, it creates an inconsistency in the API contract and can silently mask client-side bugs.
findings.recommendation: Add the same regex guard at the top of the DELETE handler before running the statement: `if (!/^\d{4}-\d{2}$/.test(key)) return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid month key (expected YYYY-MM)' });`

findings.title: No format validation on recipient address before invoking email providers
findings.description: The `/api/email/send` endpoint checks only that `to` is truthy (`if (!to)`), then passes it directly to provider API calls and SMTP. Malformed or deliberately crafted recipient strings (e.g. embedded newlines for header injection) could trigger unexpected behaviour in the nodemailer SMTP path or provider APIs. Nodemailer's address parser handles header injection internally, but explicit validation provides defence-in-depth and significantly better error UX.
findings.recommendation: Validate the `to` field against a basic email regex (e.g. `/^[^@\s]+@[^@\s]+\.[^@\s]+$/`) and reject clearly invalid values with a 400 response before any email sending logic is reached.

findings.title: buildEmailHtml is a 200-line monolith mixing HTML assembly with payment-method branching
findings.description: In emailTemplate.js, `buildEmailHtml` does everything sequentially in one function: validates the accent colour, constructs payment CTA HTML for four different providers (~60 lines of nested conditionals and template literals), maps bill rows to HTML, assembles a ~150-line full HTML document, and generates the plain-text fallback. The payment CTA section alone contains four distinct branches of deeply nested template strings. This makes it difficult to test payment-method logic in isolation, preview a single section, or modify one provider without risk of inadvertently affecting another.
findings.recommendation: Extract at minimum two helpers: (1) `buildPaymentCta(payMethod, payId, customZelleUrl, accentColor, total)` returning the `payButtonHtml` string; (2) `buildBillRows(bills, accentColor)` returning the bill-row HTML. Optionally, extract `buildEmailText(opts)` for the plain-text path. This reduces `buildEmailHtml` to a thin orchestrator and makes each section independently testable.

findings.title: JSON parse try/catch fallback pattern duplicated six times in server.js
findings.description: The pattern `try { JSON.parse(row.value/data/config) } catch { fallback }` appears at least six times across server.js: in `GET /api/state`, `GET /api/months`, `GET /api/months/:key`, `GET /api/export` (twice), `GET /api/email/config`, and `PUT /api/email/config`. Each copy uses a slightly different fallback (`{}`, `null`, or silent skip), making error-handling behaviour inconsistent and scattered. Any change to logging or fallback semantics must be applied in multiple places.
findings.recommendation: Extract a single utility at the top of server.js: `function parseJson(str, fallback = null) { try { return JSON.parse(str); } catch { return fallback; } }`. Replace all six inline try/catch blocks with calls to this helper. This also provides a single location to add diagnostic logging for parse failures.

findings.title: post() and postForm() duplicate identical response-handling boilerplate
findings.description: In email.js, `post` and `postForm` are structurally identical: both call `fetch`, await `res.text()`, throw on `!res.ok`, and return the text. The only differences are the Content-Type header and body serialisation method (`JSON.stringify` vs `URLSearchParams`). Any future change to error formatting, timeout handling (see F-002), or retry logic requires editing both functions.
findings.recommendation: Merge into a single helper: `async function httpPost(url, headers, body, asForm = false)` that sets the Content-Type and serialises the body based on the `asForm` flag. All four provider dispatch functions then call this one helper. This is also the natural place to add the timeout signal from F-002.

review.summary: BillHive is a compact, well-structured three-file Node.js billing application, but carries five critical issues and a cluster of meaningful warnings. The most severe problems are: (1) proxy authentication headers are unconditionally trusted with no enforcement mechanism, making the entire access-control model bypassable by any direct client; (2) the HTML email sanitizer's `allowedAttributes: false` setting silently passes all event-handler attributes through, undermining the protection it appears to offer; (3) SMTP TLS certificate verification is disabled for every connection by default, exposing credentials and email content to interception; (4) the email template builder crashes with a TypeError on unvalidated input types from the API; and (5) all outbound email provider HTTP calls have no timeouts, allowing a hung upstream to stall the server indefinitely. Beyond the criticals, notable warnings include SSE connection hygiene (no empty-Set pruning, no heartbeat, no per-user connection cap), the import endpoint bypassing month-key format validation enforced elsewhere, raw provider exception messages leaked to API clients, and the rate limiter accidentally applied twice to the SPA fallback route. Several low-effort code quality improvements — extracting a `parseJson` helper, splitting `buildEmailHtml` into focused sub-functions, and merging the duplicate `post`/`postForm` helpers — would meaningfully reduce maintenance overhead.

perspective_summaries.robustness: Focused on crash paths and operational resilience. Identified the two most urgent runtime issues: the type-unsafe email template builder (F-001) and the absence of outbound HTTP timeouts (F-002). Also flagged the unconditionally trusted proxy headers (F-003), SSE memory leak (F-006), globally disabled SMTP TLS (F-005), unvalidated import endpoint (F-007), and the DELETE vs PUT month-key inconsistency (F-012).
perspective_summaries.simplicity: Focused on code structure, duplication, and correctness. Uniquely identified the rate limiter double-application bug (F-011), the duplicated JSON parse try/catch pattern (F-015), the `buildEmailHtml` monolith (F-014), and the `post`/`postForm` boilerplate duplication (F-016). Also independently confirmed the sanitize-html misconfiguration (F-004), the bill amount crash (F-001), SMTP TLS issue (F-005), SSE leak (F-006), and import validation gap (F-007).
perspective_summaries.security: Focused on attack surface and data exposure. Uniquely identified the `data:` URI scheme in the sanitizer (F-008), raw exception messages leaked to clients (F-009), unlimited SSE connections enabling DoS (F-010), and missing recipient email format validation (F-013). Also independently confirmed the spoofable proxy headers (F-003), SMTP TLS bypass (F-005), sanitize-html event-handler pass-through (F-004), and import month-key bypass (F-007) — with the most detailed analysis for each.
